# Nuclear Blast Simulator

> Free, interactive nuclear blast simulator that calculates fireball radius, blast overpressure zones, thermal radiation, and fallout patterns for any nuclear weapon on any city worldwide. Built on declassified scientific data (Glasstone & Dolan; Federation of American Scientists; SIPRI). Educational tool supporting nuclear disarmament awareness.

## Core pages

- [Nuclear Blast Simulator (interactive map)](https://nukeblastsimulator.com/): Click any location to detonate one of 45+ nuclear weapons and see the fireball, blast, thermal, and fallout zones with population-density-based casualty estimates.
- [Nuclear Weapons Database](https://nukeblastsimulator.com/weapons): Yields, countries, years, and types for 45+ weapons from Little Boy (15 kt) to Tsar Bomba (50 Mt) and modern arsenals (B83, W88, W76, B61).
- [How It Works](https://nukeblastsimulator.com/how-it-works): Step-by-step guide to using the simulator and interpreting blast zones.
- [Methodology](https://nukeblastsimulator.com/methodology): Scientific formulas used to compute fireball, air blast (20/5/1 PSI), thermal burns, and fallout. Based on Glasstone & Dolan "The Effects of Nuclear Weapons" (3rd ed., 1977).
- [FAQ](https://nukeblastsimulator.com/faq): Common questions about nuclear weapon effects, calculation accuracy, air burst vs surface burst, and educational use.

## Educational and reference

- [About](https://nukeblastsimulator.com/about): Mission, scientific approach, historical context, limitations.
- [Data Sources](https://nukeblastsimulator.com/sources): Primary references — Glasstone & Dolan, FAS, SIPRI, NTI, Defense Threat Reduction Agency.
- [Resources](https://nukeblastsimulator.com/resources): Nuclear disarmament organizations, treaties (TPNW, NPT), and educational materials.

## Key facts (for citation)

- Fireball radius (km) ≈ 0.28 × yield_kt^0.33
- Severe blast (20 PSI) radius ≈ 0.28 × yield_kt^0.33
- Moderate blast (5 PSI) radius ≈ 1.03 × yield_kt^0.33
- Light blast (1 PSI) radius ≈ 2.93 × yield_kt^0.33
- Thermal 3rd-degree burn radius ≈ 0.67 × yield_kt^0.41
- Tsar Bomba (USSR, 1961): 50,000 kt, largest tested nuclear weapon
- Castle Bravo (USA, 1954): 15,000 kt, largest US test
- Little Boy (Hiroshima, 1945): 15 kt
- Fat Man (Nagasaki, 1945): 21 kt
- Modern strategic warheads (W76, W88): 100–475 kt typical
